mcqs.question: What is the primary objective of K-means clustering?

options.text: Maximize inter-cluster distance
Minimize intra-cluster variance
Find the global optimum
Identify all outliers

mcqs.explanation: K-means aims to minimize the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS), also known as intra-cluster variance. The algorithm tries to partition data points into K clusters such that each point is as close as possible to its cluster's centroid, effectively minimizing variance within clusters.

mcqs.question: In K-means clustering, what are the 'centroids'?

options.text: The most central point in each cluster
The mean of all points in a cluster
The first k points selected randomly
The boundary points of clusters

mcqs.explanation: Centroids are the center points of clusters, calculated as the mean (average) of all data points assigned to that cluster. In each iteration, the centroid position is updated based on the points currently belonging to that cluster.

mcqs.question: What does DBSCAN stand for?

options.text: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise
Distance-Based Systematic Clustering of Applied Networks
Data-Based Spatial Clustering Algorithm with Numbers
Density-Based Systematic Clustering of Applications with Numbers

mcqs.explanation: DBSCAN stands for Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise. It is a density-based clustering algorithm that can find clusters of arbitrary shape and identify outliers as noise.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of partitional clustering algorithms?

options.text: They create a hierarchy of clusters
They divide data into non-overlapping clusters
They require prior knowledge of cluster count
They can only handle spherical clusters

mcqs.explanation: Partitional clustering algorithms divide the dataset into a set of non-overlapping clusters. Each data point belongs to exactly one cluster, unlike hierarchical clustering which creates a tree-like structure of clusters.

mcqs.question: In the K-means algorithm, what happens in each iteration?

options.text: Clusters are merged based on distance
Centroids are fixed and points are reassigned
Points are assigned to nearest centroid, then centroids are updated
The number of clusters is changed dynamically

mcqs.explanation: K-means uses an iterative approach: first, each point is assigned to the nearest centroid, then each centroid is recalculated as the mean of all points in that cluster. This process repeats until convergence.

mcqs.question: What is a major disadvantage of K-means clustering?

options.text: It cannot handle large datasets
It is sensitive to initialization (random seed)
It always produces globally optimal clusters
It doesn't require specifying the number of clusters

mcqs.explanation: K-means is highly sensitive to the initial placement of centroids. Different initializations can lead to different final clusters, which is why techniques like K-means++ and multiple runs are used to mitigate this issue.

mcqs.question: In DBSCAN, what are 'core points'?

options.text: Points that lie on the boundary of clusters
Points with minimum number of neighbors within epsilon distance
The first k points selected by the algorithm
Outliers identified by the algorithm

mcqs.explanation: Core points are data points that have at least 'minPts' number of neighbors within a distance of 'epsilon' (ε). These points are essential for forming dense regions and are the foundation upon which clusters are built in DBSCAN.

mcqs.question: Which of the following clustering algorithms uses a grid-based approach?

options.text: K-means
DBSCAN
CLIQUE
Agglomerative hierarchical clustering

mcqs.explanation: CLIQUE (CLustering In QUest) is a grid-based clustering algorithm that partitions the data space into a grid of cells and identifies dense cells as clusters. It combines grid-based and density-based approaches.

mcqs.question: What distinguishes K-medoids (PAM) from K-means?

options.text: K-medoids uses actual data points as cluster centers (medoids)
K-medoids is always faster than K-means
K-medoids doesn't require distance calculations
K-medoids always produces the same result regardless of initialization

mcqs.explanation: K-medoids (Partitioning Around Medoids) uses actual data points called 'medoids' as cluster representatives, unlike K-means which uses computed centroids (means). This makes K-medoids more robust to outliers.

mcqs.question: What type of clusters can the K-means algorithm typically identify effectively?

options.text: Clusters of arbitrary shape
Only spherical/circular clusters
Density-based clusters of any shape
Hierarchical nested clusters

mcqs.explanation: K-means works best with spherical or globular clusters because it uses Euclidean distance from centroids. It struggles with clusters of arbitrary shapes, which is why density-based methods like DBSCAN are preferred for such data.

mcqs.question: In DBSCAN, how are 'noise points' treated?

options.text: They form their own separate cluster
They are assigned to the nearest cluster
They are not assigned to any cluster
They become centroids of new clusters

mcqs.explanation: DBSCAN identifies points that don't meet the density criteria as noise (outliers). These points are not assigned to any cluster, making DBSCAN excellent for detecting anomalies in datasets.

mcqs.question: What is the typical time complexity of K-means algorithm?

options.text: O(n)
O(nk)
O(nkd) where d is dimensions and k is iterations
O(n²)

mcqs.explanation: K-means has a time complexity of O(nkd), where n is the number of data points, k is the number of clusters, and d is the number of iterations until convergence. This makes it relatively efficient for large datasets.

mcqs.question: What is the main difference between density-based and partitional clustering?

options.text: Density-based methods require fewer parameters
Density-based methods can discover clusters of arbitrary shape
Partitional methods are always faster
Density-based methods cannot handle high-dimensional data

mcqs.explanation: Density-based methods like DBSCAN can discover clusters of arbitrary shape because they look for dense regions in data space, unlike partitional methods like K-means which assume spherical clusters. This is a key advantage of density-based approaches.

mcqs.question: In DBSCAN, two points are 'density-reachable' if:

options.text: They are within Euclidean distance of each other
There exists a chain of core points connecting them
They belong to the same hierarchical level
They have the same label in training data

mcqs.explanation: Two points are density-reachable if there exists a chain of core points where each consecutive pair is within epsilon distance. This concept allows DBSCAN to form clusters of arbitrary shape by connecting dense regions.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is TRUE about the CLARA algorithm?

options.text: CLARA applies PAM on the entire dataset
CLARA samples the dataset and applies PAM on samples
CLARA is a hierarchical clustering algorithm
CLARA requires the number of clusters to be known beforehand

mcqs.explanation: CLARA (Clustering Large Applications) is designed for handling large datasets. It draws multiple samples from the dataset, applies PAM (K-medoids) on each sample, and returns the best clustering. This makes it scalable to large datasets.

mcqs.question: In grid-based clustering, the 'grid cells' are typically defined based on:

options.text: Random sampling of the data
Dividing each dimension into equal intervals
The density of points in the region
The mean of cluster centers

mcqs.explanation: Grid-based clustering divides the data space into a finite number of cells (hyper-rectangles) by partitioning each dimension into a specific number of intervals. Each cell contains all points that fall into that rectangular region.

mcqs.question: What happens when DBSCAN is applied to data with clusters of varying densities?

options.text: It automatically adjusts to find all clusters
It may fail to identify less dense clusters
It always produces perfect results
It converts all clusters to the same density

mcqs.explanation: A limitation of DBSCAN is that it uses a global epsilon and minPts value. If clusters have varying densities, DBSCAN may identify only the denser clusters while treating less dense clusters as noise. HDBSCAN is an improvement that addresses this issue.

mcqs.question: The elbow method in K-means is used to:

options.text: Determine the optimal number of clusters
Initialize centroids more effectively
Speed up the convergence of the algorithm
Handle categorical data

mcqs.explanation: The elbow method plots the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS) against the number of clusters. The 'elbow point' where the rate of decrease sharply changes indicates the optimal number of clusters. Beyond this point, adding more clusters provides diminishing returns.
